title: 2025 12 10 FEC
displayTitle: 2025-12-10 - Finance and Expenditure Committee - Inquiry into performance reporting and public accountability
topic: Inquiry into performance reporting and public accountability
description: Wednesday 10 December 2025 | Finance and Expenditure Committee Inquiry into performance reporting and public accountability External Reporting Board John Kensington, Chairperson Wendy Ventner, Chief Executive Michelle Lombaard, Director, Accounting Standards Financial Markets Conduct Amendment Bill - Amendment Paper 446 Chartered Accountants Australia and New Zealand Karen McWilliams, Sustainability leader Lydia Tsen, New Zealand Government Affairs leader Chapman Tripp Roger Wallis, Partner Financial Services Council Aimie Hines, General Manager Financial Services Council
descriptionSummary: Inquiry into performance reporting and public accountability. External Reporting Board. John Kensington, Chairperson

chunks.text: Okay. Thank you, thank you for your patience. We've got an allocated 20 minutes there, but we'll, we're also conscious that we've, um, got hea- hearings a- after you. Yeah. So we're in your hands. Very good, very good. We actually had our chair joining us, um, on Zoom- Yeah, I think he's just joined. Um, so I think he's there. He, he's gonna start off with a few introductory remarks and then over to me, but yeah.

Okay. Have we got Mr. Kensington on, online yet? Ah, yes we do. Good morning. Oh, Mr. Kensington. For the, for the benefit of the live audience, if you could al- ah, introduce you and your colleagues and your organization as well. Thank you. For sure, certainly. Good morning, Chair and committee members, I'm John Kensington. I am the Chair of the External, uh, Reporting Board, or XRB as it's often referred to. I'm joined by Wendy, uh, Venter, our Chief Executive, and Michelle Lombard, our Director of Accounting Standards in the

room with you. And we're an independent community, um, that is responsible for setting, uh, external reporting and assurance standards. Um, and look, thank you for the opportunity to contribute to this important inquiry. We see this as a rare, uh, and timely chance to improve New Zealand's performance reporting system to deliver lasting public benefit.

Now, New Zealand's public sector financial reporting is internationally recognized and robust, but our non-financial, uh, performance reporting has not kept pace. Uh, large volumes of information are produced, yet Parliament and the public don't consistently receive a clear and integrated view of what government aims to achieve, how money is used to achieve it, and what process is being made, particularly on long-term end it- uh, issues.

As an independent Crown entity, and the national, uh, external reporting and assurance standard-setter working in this sometimes complicated space, this is our core business. The XRB sets fit for purpose reporting and assurance standards across our entire economy. The public, the private, and the not-for-profit sectors to deliver consistent, trustworthy information that users need. Recently, 83% of our, um, stakeholders noted they were very satisfied with the XRB's contribution towards that purpose. Now, if you were to use a house building

analogy, you wouldn't build one without all the required tradies being involved. And as you build this new performance framework, you probably should, uh, and we probably should be seen as one of those tradies alongside Treasury, the OAG, and others. We welcome the system change and the opportunity to play a part in the reform process, and contributing to the evolution and enhancement of this reporting is one of our strategic priorities.

What we'll d- do, if you don't mind, is take the p- position as read and then get Wendy, who's in the room, to briefly highlight some of those main points. Um, good. And thank- thank you, John. And- and my thanks to the committee for this, um, opportunity to appear. We very much welcome it. Uh, I just wanted to make some key points and really (coughing drowns out speaker) leave time for any questions that you might have. Um, first point is really just, um, to, like to mention some key concepts that underpin all our external

reporting frameworks and standards, which we believe are sort of relevant for this inquiry. Um, and then sort of talk about the key opportunities for reform that we see. The starting point for any external reporting frameworks is user needs. And the users in this context are obviously Parliament and the public. Um, and the other point is, we really sort of strongly emphasize is that reporting is not an end in itself, it's there to serve a purpose. The information that is produced should be useful and used. Um, we recommend therefore that the, uh, committee as it, as it works its way through

this next phase of the inquiry really just focus on, strongly focus on user needs. Uh, because they are diverse, wide-ranging, and trying to be specific as possible, uh, in terms of meeting the purpose. We agree with the, the key purpose, um, the inter- the committee's interim report that the purpose of the system is to provide information that enables accountability, improvement, and trust.

Uh, we also suggest that information should inform decision-making. This is a key conceptual underpinning of all external reporting frameworks that should also inform, um, decisions. Is it decision useful information, um, for Parliament, the public, and indeed the executive? I just briefly wanna talk about four key opportunities for reform that we see.

I've sort of talked about the why, uh, was, well, what are we trying to achieve? The next two are really about the what information. Better meeting user needs for outcome-focused long-term information. The committee's report has very clearly highlighted that as being a key gap, uh, and we would agree. This will require quite different thinking about, uh, the different levels of reporting. Is it sector level?

Is it whole of government level? What do you need at an appropriation level? There are also opportunities to leverage other frameworks. So that's one of the what's. The second what we do see as a big opportunity here is a simplification of the, of the reporting landscape, which once again has been highlighted in the committee's int- interim report. The landscape is cluttered and complex.

Um, balancing user needs, cost, and benefit will be absolutely crucial to the committee reaching its, uh, views about the recommendations that you want to make. Is there an opportunity to think about more targeted, more tailored information that might be more efficient and effective? Um, so that's the second what. The- the other key opportunity, which once again is highlighted in the committee's report as enabler, is the how. Investing in the capability required to report non-financial information well.

This includes the people, planning and evaluation frameworks, the data, and we would emphasize the modern technology platforms. Um-We would not underestimate the size of the, the effort involved in getting the capability revived and I think the lessons from previous reforms offer a blueprint there of how, what might, you might not want to do and what you might want to, uh, do. Uh, it is something we, as standard setters, are spending an increasing amount of time on supporting the implementation.

Lastly, thinking about the assurance that you require. Um, there is a different, there's, you know, the world of assurance is evolving internationally, locally, there's a mix of di-, um, assurance products- (coughs)... that fit the nature of reporting. What do you absolutely want to have trust in? What type of trust? Um, so I think that's an- another area that we would absolutely endorse, um, looking at.

Finally, just sort of repeating a little bit of what, uh, John said, we're really well-placed to support the committee's work and welcome the opportunity to do so. We have system-wide perspectives, um, technical capability and established stakeholder engagement processes, well-connected internationally and locally, uh, with the Treasury, the OAG, uh, locally, regulators, reporting entities, um, international s- bodies, other jurisdictions, Australia, Europe, UK, Canada, you name it. Um, and as I said, we do support

implementation so we do have some experience really about what are the ways in which you might phase change, how do you support everybody participating in the system. I'll stop there, uh, and, um, those are the key points we wanted to highlight, Chair, and very, um, you know, welcome any questions that the committee might have. Oh, very good. Thank you. Yeah. Uh, we're gonna go to Dr. Wilson and then we'll go to Dr.

Woods- And thank you.... and, and Dr. Russell. Okay. Very interesting that you're, you've got a seeming dichotomy between financial and non-financial. Mm-hmm. Um, and of course, one of the key tools that we use for investment and whether it's any policy- Yeah.... agency or government entity is- Yeah.... investment, money.

Yeah. Um, (clears throat) so could you just explain how you will draw those concepts together in terms of the new frameworks that you're thinking about? Yeah. Because, you know, those things are completely related in a lot of ways. Yeah. And it makes a big difference to- Yeah.... how you explain it. Yeah. No, thank you. Uh, and that's a excellent point. I think in, in terms of we talk about it as being, well the, the money is there to enable people to do things, whether- Yeah.

... it's, um, and, um, at, uh, a really simple level. So thinking about financial and non-financial performance and, and the frameworks that we use are actually fundamental to, you know, it is, uh, here's the cost and, and is it producing the right benefit? Um, we do have a, the service performance, uh, reporting framework that underpins the reporting and the public benefit entity, which is both the, uh, public sector and the not-for-profit that has obviously underpinned some of that reporting at the entity level.

Um, I think our experience would say that the frameworks themselves, and some recent consultation that we've done in the not-for-profit sector, that the frameworks and the thinking are there but people are not actually, as you say, don't quite have the muscle memory, don't have the capability to think about actually what is truly good measures of performance.

That's where the struggle often happens. Mm-hmm. There is also, in terms of other frameworks, um, which we would, um, which we have highlighted in our submission, is the sort of what we, you know, is often call integrated reported, reporting, and integrated thinking. And I think the committee's advisors have drawn some of those to the, your, your attention to do. Which are not, to me, if you stand back from this, is, um, it, it all drives from good governance, good management, good focus on, on what performance looks like to you, whether it's financial performance or

non-financial performance. So some of those integrated reporting frameworks, um, do sort of encourage that thinking to happen within the organization. So those are probably a couple of the key points I'd make. Yeah. Because I think the- Yeah.... the key question that the public's gonna be asking and so will we is- Yeah.... "Okay, we've invested this much and we wanted these outcomes. How did that work and why?"

Yeah. And in what respect? David, can you bring the microphone closer to your mouth? It's hard to hear you. Sure. Yeah. You, did you hear that okay, Megan? Mm-hmm. What I just said? Yep. No, I just- Yeah, I think you had your hand up too. Yeah. Yeah, um, yeah. No, ab- absolutely. I think that's, uh, I, I think that's at the fundamental heart of our framework or something. I might invite Michelle to- Okay.

... comment on that because, uh, we do, we do hear internationally some of these same, trying to draw the connections- Yeah. ... whether it's sustainability information, if it's climate reporting, whatever you might do, these are con- conversations happening internationally too, is how do you make sure that you are getting good information about the organization, whether, no matter which, um, sector they're in.

Yeah. And you're asking the question- Yeah.... whether or not we invest more in this area, program- Yeah.... project, whatever it is, what sort of outcomes are we likely to get? Yeah. Yeah. Absolutely. Okay. Right, we're gonna go to Dr. Woods. Thank you. Um, and thank you very much for your submission. Um, really useful. The thing I'm interested in is the utilization of these reports.

Yeah. So we can produce all the reports we want, the question is how the executive or the parliament uses them in terms of, um, measuring per- the performance and particularly their non-financial. Yeah. What do you see as being some of the critical interfaces with the executive? Yeah. Um, is it through DPMC, so feeding into something like a cabinet prio- a Cabinet Priorities Committee framework to measure their performance? Is it, um, a supplementary report?

Select committees get reports from the Auditor-General, uh, when we, when we do our scrutiny. Do you see feeding into that? Where do you see those critical interfaces being? Um, yeah, that's a great question. Um, I'd, hmm, I would... As a start off thing, I think the answer will be that there's probably a few things to sort of work through in there. But my, um, one of the key things which has come up, the, is around the sectors. Like, what are the, uh, what- what's the nature of the information that's g- will be most useful, um, for

select committees? Mm-hmm. You would be thinking about how select committees are organized, what sort of information that, uh, is relevant for each committee. Then I think there is a case of actually working through the accountability, uh, that individual agencies have. What's the role of central agencies?Um, I think the committee's... You know, there has been information that you've, I think, in your report which sort of says, really starting from that enduring outcomes, what are the priority objectives? For us, a really crucial piece of that is really

getting a clarity on what are the objectives of the reporting, and then the structures and the right format of the reporting will, will flow, will flow from that. So it doesn't... I think you are a- asking a little bit about how the executive works, uh, which may be, you know, might be Treasury to, or the OAG, to sort of advise on, on that, um, through your chair. But I, I don't think there is a lot of information produced.

Yeah. Um, some of it is core accountability information. Information is produced in other forms. So, I think, to, to my mind, is sort of a lot of the thinking will be in actually the design of simplifying what is the most useful information, and then, to be honest the, the, the system could work to produce that information. It is, it is re- really about, um, focusing on the purpose. But yeah, I'm not sure I fully answered your question, but I think that's- Yeah.

... that's my, my sort of best way of, o- off addressing what you were, what I think you were trying to, to get at. I guess, yeah, one of the things that I'm interested in is- Yeah. Yeah. ... um, if we have good reporting- Yeah.... we may well, um, d- discover some red flags- Yeah.... um, that need, that need to be considered- Yeah.... either by the executive, or Parliament, or both.

Yeah. Yeah. How do we ensure that happens? A lot of reporting is produced across all of government. Mm-hmm. How do we ensure that this actually has a way to actually b- being utilized so we can see early on in a program- Yeah.... in terms of those non-financial performance measures that it may not be working? Yeah.

Yeah. Yeah. Did you... No, uh, it looks like Michelle's wanting to say something, so, uh, yeah. I always say reporting is the end means, right? Yeah. And that's what you get at the back end. It's also about how the intervention logic or the thinking processes work and how it stacks up before you get to the reporting itself. So that's where you do think about the assurance products and how that- Yeah.

... you can leverage through that, where you potentially, um, bring in assurance, different types of assurance earlier in the piece, and making sure those entities are actually able to report correctly. Yeah. I think there's an element of that, and then at the back end when the reporting actually happens, um, as Wendy has said, being clear on what the information is for what purpose is quite critical in this piece because bringing it back to framing up what's in that report then is easier to identify in making those clear links.

Um, one more thing I would say is the proportionate reporting. Uh, I think we have a fixed mindset about annual reporting. Yeah. And maybe that's not necessarily the requirement for non-financial information. There's potentially a piece here where you really think about or really criticize that and think, well, from a non-financial perspective, what does this information look like and how often are entities having to report?

Yeah. And what level of reporting is, what level of entities is required? Yeah. I don't think every single le- well, my opinion is I don't think every single level of entity needs to be reporting on non-financial information within government. Thank you. Very good. Uh, Dr. Russell? So, um, we've been talking about reporting financial information and, and i- it's really useful in terms of the annual reports and that part of the reporting cycle for government entities. But a whole set of the opaqueness that I find, um, even as an accountant, comes with the budget

reporting. Right. And it's the way that we talk about what, what expenditure is planned, where the money is sourced from, whether it's an increase or a decrease. And I'm, you know, I'm an accountant and I find those damn things hard to read. Yeah. Um, so have you given any thought to that part of our reporting cycle? Yeah. Um, yes, I think it, it is part of the entire picture, right?

It comes back to what's the setting. Um, and actually in my, one of my prior lives I spent probably, well, a good... Many months in the Auditor-General's office advising this committee, I should say. So, uh, for my sins had to, um, had to sort of look through a lot of budget documents, so I would agree with that, that it is, it is complex. Um, making the connections right at that start, from the budget process. What, you know, what are we trying to achieve?

Where's the money going? What are the measures? I think there's an element which had been drawn out in the committee's report. So a lot of those, these problems have been identified as, do you want information at the appropriation level or do you need it at the outcome level? Um, simplifying that aspect of it will also be, be helpful. Thinking about how technology plays a part in giving you ready access to the right information to navigate, because often these things have to be built up, right, through a complicate- uh,

complicated system. Uh, but how do you get to a point where, uh, committees, select committees, this committee, can actually sort of easily sort of engage on the right information sets that are useful for whatever it is that you're scrutinizing. Um, so yeah, definitely opportunities to... I see it as, we see it as part of the entire system of reporting.

Yeah. Okay. Uh, Mr. Simonson? Oh, well, I was just gonna... I think that kind of actually- Yeah. ... answers a bit of what I was... So, one, I want to say thanks for your very detailed submission. You're actually our first, uh, submitters on this since we put out our... Well, to appear before us for an interim report. Very helpful. Yeah, I really just wanted to call out, I think your point about user needs.

Mm-hmm. Government is very good at just creating a system that suits, I don't know, the system, rather than actually thinking about the end user. And I think, again, I think... Thank you for offering. It may be we want to come back to you at some point and test some of these things around that user needs. Because I think if we don't get that right- Yeah.

... everything else is going to be wasted. Thank you. That's... That was a great point to finish on. I would, I would have em- emphasized that. So we very much appreciate your time and thank you for Okay. No, thank you, uh, to the chief executive and to the chair, uh, and to the, um, director. Thank you so much for coming in, uh, on behalf of the External Reporting Board, uh, for our Performance Reporting and Public Inquir- uh, Accountability Inquiry Hearings.

We know you'll be watching. Members, members, we have got a couple more next week. Uh, we've got the public sector one that's going to be fronted by the ch- chief executive of IR and the Ministry for Women, and we've got an individual submission as well. So, um, that's something to look forward to next week. Right, that brings us... Thank you so much. (clears throat) That brings us to the Chartered Accountants Australia and New Zealand for our next, uh, segment.

... on the Financial Markets Conduct Amendment Bill. Uh- And this is on the removing the climate stuff, isn't it? Well, this is on the changing the climate disclosure thresholds- Disclosure.... um, that the minister, Scott Simpson, um, has put out an amendment paper and, uh, subsequently we called for submissions and we are now hearing submissions today.

Uh, as of now, we've got Chartered Accountants Australia in New Zealand, and we've also got submissions, just for the benefit of our live audience, um, from 1:00 this afternoon. You'll also note the previous, um, firms have written in support of CAANZ's, uh, submission, uh, and so we look forward to your presentation.

Welcome. Thank you. Um, and thank you for the opportunity to appear today. Uh, my name is Karen McWilliams and I'm the sustainability leader at Chartered Accountants Australia New Zealand, and I'm joined by my colleague Lydia Sen, who's the New Zealand government affairs leader. We're the professional body for chartered accountants and have 31,000 members here in New Zealand. Um, CAANZ advocates in the public good on behalf of our members, and our members play a critical role in preparing and assuring climate-related disclosures.

We have closely engaged in the climate-related disclosure regime since the initial consultations began in 2019 and are supporting the ongoing implementation. We provided a submission to MBIE at the beginning of this year in response to their discussion document on adjustments to the regime. Better disclosure facilitates more informed decision-making and makes international investment to support, I'm sorry, and drives international investment to support New Zealand's economic growth. We understand there are concerns from reporting entities about the costs

incurred during these initial years of disclosure. However, the reporting entities have now built those skills and processes needed to meet the current requirements, and we're beginning to see the shift to more strategic benefits. The proposed changes to increase the reporting thresholds will reduce the effectiveness of New Zealand's climate-related disclosures regime at a time when global expectations in investor demand for this information are increasing.

So thank you, Chair, and we welcome the committee's questions. Oh, very good. Okay. Questions, I've got Dr. Russell. Oh. Mm-hmm. Um, thank you. (laughs) Appreciate the brevity of your submission and the very direct points there. I have been discussing these issues with various, um, climate-related entities and one of the suggestions from them has been that even if we do just these, um, the way we do it in New Zealand, perhaps doing, just aligning ourselves with what Australia does would be helpful, um, in, in terms of

their climate-related disclosures. Do you have a view on that? Um, well I think it's important to note that, that Australia's regime, um, will bring in approximately 8,000 entities over the next two to three years. Um, so the threshold in Australia's regime is the same as for financial reporting, um, so it takes it down to roughly 50 million turnover and 25 million assets under management or 100 employees, so you meet two out of those three. Um, it also includes asset managers and unlisted entities. So I think New Zealand's original sort of thresholds are

probably more aligned with where Australia will end up, albeit that there's a difference in the phasing and the timing. Um, so I guess the proposed amendments in New Zealand will probably reduce the, the number of entities reporting now to under 80, so that's quite a distinction there. Um, also should mention that, um, uh, Australia's regime will include unlisted entities as well.

Um, so there are quite some, with the proposed thresholds here to remove, um, ex-asset managers and increase the threshold to one billion market capitalization, we're looking at quite different, um, thresholds and number of entities that will be reporting. So we're moving into a situation where, um, we are reporting much less than our, our major trading partner.

Yeah. Um, and I would imagine too a lot of the firms here, um, well, um, plenty of the, the, uh, Australian-owned entities here would have to report enough information to enable their Australian parents to report accurately as well anyway. Yeah. There wi- there will be some information, um, I guess, sharing around that, um, that will certainly sort of take place.

Um, but I think it's certainly clear that, that, um, that there's a difference in the phasing, but the ultimate, um, sort of end thresholds are now gonna be quite, quite different. Right. Okay. Okay. Dr. Woods? Thank you. And thank you very much for your submission. I'm really interested in the remarks you made around the implications and impact on investment.

Um, we're hearing through another, a number of submissions that the fragmentan- fragmented inconsistencies creates uncertainty and that that can go on to affect valuations, discount rates, s- investment strategy, and ultimately the competitiveness of New Zealand companies seeking capital at home and abroad.

So I'd just like to, to hear a bit more from you around what the consequences in terms of investment could be for New Zealand. Yeah, so I guess we are seeing very strongly globally, um, demand for this type of information, um, and, um, that sort of, and that coming from the capital markets and coming from investors. Um, so obviously, um, the absence of New Zealand companies preparing this information, um, means that investors will be having to make their own assumptions around the associated climate risks and opportunities in relation to New Zealand entities, and those assumptions may well

be incorrect. Um, so by not providing that information and informing the market accurately, um, those investors will make assumptions. And of course, typically if you don't have the information, you're gonna be relatively more conservative than you might be if you had, um, information. So I think that's the impact that we're certainly gonna see, um, which is there's gonna be an assumption that New Zealand companies are much greater exposed to, to risks than perhaps they are in the absence of information. And that will therefore have knock-on effects,

um, for the, that provider of finance. Thank you. Okay, uh, Dr. Russell- So just— just following on from that, um, can you see a situation where even if this information is not required by law, that potential investors or stock markets or other regulators, if you like, if, even if they're private, um, you know, um, not government regulators, um, might demand the information anyway? I'm just trying to think here.

You know, audit- auditing started because investors demanded it, not because governments required it. Mm. I mean, certainly, um, I guess what we've seen globally is that, um, there were increasing demand, and I guess we've seen increasing demand for climate disclosures in a voluntary capacity. I think the challenge is that those existing voluntary frameworks were not sufficient to meet the information needs of investors and, and providers of capital. So what they're really looking for is ensuring that entities are reporting to the same standards, disclosing the same information, make it, making

it consistent and comparable. Um- So I guess that's the a- that's what was missing in the voluntary regime, and we did see a lot of stock markets around the world bringing it into, bringing the T- CFDs, the Task Force for Climate-related Financial Disclosures. They were bringing that into their listing rules, um, as, as a requirement through that, um, but it wasn't really sufficient, I guess, from an, a global investor perspective, and that's where we've seen that drive for- Okay. ... regulated standards.

Thank, thank you so much, uh, to, to CANZ, the Chartered Accountants of Australia and New Zealand, for your submission. Mm-hmm. I'm keen to give everyone 10 minutes this morning 'cause we're on a hard finish, so thank you. We appreciate. Great submission. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Okay, that takes us to, um, Chapman Tripp, uh, who are also online with, you've handed out a supplementary, um, submission in hard copy form.

Raja, very good to see you. Well, thank you. Uh, welcome. Good, good morning. Welcome to our, uh- Um- ... hearings on the, on the- Morning.... amendment paper. We're in your hands. You've got 10 minutes. Sure. Um, I plan to keep this reasonably brief, um, 'cause I can take the written, uh, submission as read.

Don't plan to, uh, get into a lot of detail about the reason for the bill. Uh, broadly, we're supportive of it. Um, we've practiced in this area since inception, uh, and worked along with our clients. It's simply proved to be a little bit too cumbersome, um, in that the benefits of the regime have not yet, um, matched the cost of them. I think the regulatory impact statement that was attached to the Cabinet paper recommending the reset of the threshold sets out nicely the, um, various considerations around that.

Um, I, I guess the central theme of some of the written submissions are that one of the mistakes made in designing this regime at the outset was to try and treat climate statements as if they were financial statements. Mm. Um, so there's a lot of focus on, um, the approach taken, uh, to financial reporting, which hasn't adapted that well, um, to, to climate statement reporting. Um, one of those is the way that you measure whether you're in the regime.

In the financial reporting context, you look back a couple years and look at what the financial statements said to determine whether or not you're going to need to, uh, prepare full reports and, and if you're overseas, unfile them. Um, that hasn't really worked that well in this area because it means two things. One, you're really looking two or three years ago, in, at the values that were, um, apparent then, particularly for listed companies, um, rather than the more immediate period.

In the case of listed companies, market capitalization, which is the core threshold, is measured every single day, so it's not as if you're not gonna know whether you're likely to be, be in the regime by measuring it on a more timely basis. Correspondingly, if you think about someone that's newly listed, the way the regime's measured at the moment, you don't then become subject to the regime potentially for up to three years after you become listed.

S- so there's just a bit of, bit of mismatch there reflecting some concepts taken from the financial reporting concept that, that don't really work. One of the core, um, features of the change that we strongly support is the removal of deemed director liability, the personalization of liability for directors. E- essentially the policy approach has been to treat a set of climate statements as if they were a prospectus and so impose personal liability on the directors as well as the reporting entity itself. However, this is a very technical area where

significant consulting support is needed. The, uh, climate statements are technical, evolving, and complicated. The international standards are going in a slightly different direction. So we support the minister's, um, changes reflected in the amendment paper to center the liability only on the reporting entity rather than the individual directors and, and think that will bring several benefits. It will mean that, um, directors can focus on the more strategic matters rather than worrying about their personal risk and having to be personally involved in some quite technical

disclosures. It means that the reporting entities might be a little bit more courageous about some of the disclosures that they seek to make rather than sort of the first question being top of mind is, "Is this gonna create a liability for me?" So we strongly support the, uh, change in approach.

If I then take it back to the financial reporting analogy, the same issue arises in the context of financial statements, so our submission also submits that the commission, that the committee should go a bit further and recommend that the deemed personalization of liability be also taken away from directors for producing financial statements, again, for the same reasons.

It's become increasingly technical, complicated, uh, doesn't, um, under the current construct, allow directors to easily delegate tasks that might be better del- delegated to an audit committee or to involve, to a greater degree, investment professionals, um, rather than individual directors who can't be expected to s- have the same, uh, kind of knowledge.The third area where, again, um, drawing on financial reporting concepts hasn't worked that well, is the incredibly broad notion of climate records. In the context of preparing financial statements,

it's appropriate for accounting records to be very broadly defined and to include all of the primary evidence to make up, um, what's needed to record financial statements, invoices, purchase orders, et cetera. However, in applying that same definition in the climate statement area, it's meant that there's a very wide catchment of records that need to be kept for seven years. In theory, the FMA can turn up to reception with a notice and ask to inspect them all.

And so we submit that, uh, an alternative and preferable approach would be to take the approach the Australians have and focus on records that really go to, um, what is needed to support the disclosures made in the climate statements rather than the underlying primary records, which in many cases will be duplicative and quite comprehensive, um, when that level of, um, record keeping is unnecessary to support the core disclosures.

In terms of the supplementary submission, um, that essentially just suggests to the committee that it would be useful to put into the legislation the purpose for this regime. It was included in the general policy statement for the original bill, um, but for some reason wasn't included in the bill itself. That would be useful because I think there has been some confusion about what this regime is trying to solve for. Uh, it- it doesn't, um, seek to provide, uh, a- a comprehensive view of, uh, climate risk or- or- or climate considerations for the entire

economy because the application is essentially only to banks and very large listed companies is in fact a very small part of the economy. It's only about 12- 12% of the economy. The original purpose of the regime was to try and help investors with capital allocation decisions, and the reality is, uh, offshore investors will typically only be looking at our very large companies.

I'm Amy Hines, the general manager advocacy at the Financial Services Council. Um, the FSC welcomes proposed amendments to the Financial Markets Conduct Bill in amendment paper 446 as pragmatic recalibration of the climate-related disclosure scheme. The FSC has over 110 members, over 100 billion in funds, and paying over $2.8 billion in claims annually.

(man sneezes) Our members, as you know, are life, health, disability, income insurers, fund managers, KiwiSaver and workplace savings schemes, and professional services and technology providers. These changes proposed represent a right sizing of regulation, ensuring the framework is fit for purpose, balancing transparency, proportionality, and market competitiveness. These changes enable for the way that reducing, uh, compliance burden by raising the reporting threshold

from 60 million to 100 billion market cap, preventing small issuers from facing disproportionate costs. It also encourages market participation by removing managed investment schemes from the regime, aligns with investor feedback, and avoids unnecessary complexity. Also, it maintains robust standards. Adjusted liability se- settings for directors strike the right balance.

It preserves accountability without discouraging detailed disclosures. We note some comments in our substantive submission, although I wanted to highlight the area of the transitional provisions. Section 416T1 creates unintended consequences with entities falling outside the scope may still need to report for multiple years.

We have a simple fix for the committee to make, add tre- transitional provisions to clause 108 to clarify that these entities are not required to prepare climate reports for accounting pur- purpose areas described in clause 108, uh, one. But also, we also do in our submission, we suggest that the committee can take one step further here.

There's a further opportunity for the committee around life and health insurers. They are a current an- anomaly in the system. Life and health insurers remain captured by the CRD regime, despite their business model being fundamentally different from property or general insurers. Why exclude them? They're largely service-based organizations with minimal physical assets and materially smaller direct commissions, just like those organizations who have been proposed to be excluded. Their compliance costs are disproportionate and deliver little

meaningful climate insight. Insurers estimated the cost added to them each year is over 500K for a smaller outfit to over a million dollars plus for the larges. The regulatory focus remains on sectors where climate risk is operationally material and disclosures can drive tangible emissions and reductions. The policy alignment here, excluding life and health insurance is consistent with the policy directions of the cabinet and the current changes, which aim to reduce unnecessary burden. Um, the influ- way influence, these areas

can influence, banks in general influ- um, insurers can influence climate outcomes through lending and property insurance. Life and health insurance do not have the equivalent levers. Um, there is also existing oversight here. So RBNZ guidance under the IPSA currently requires insurers to consider climate risk.

Adding CRD obligations creates regulatory duplication without net benefit. Um, also the audin- audience here, climate standards are designed for investors and lenders, not consumers who are seeking life and health insurance. Insurers have told us that downloads of disclosures are at a bare minimum, with one lessurer telling us that there was three downloads, of which two were from their internal staff.

And from a practicality aspect, Southern Cross Medical Care Society is a climate reporting entity despite having no investors or lenders. Its climate statements assume an investor audience, which does not match its consumer base. This illustrates the disconnect between CRD obligations and the reality of life and health insurance ob- obligations.

To conclude, you know, we support the intent of the proposed changes in the amendment paper of course, but we also think that, that the select committee can go one step forward and make some recommendations in the CRD scheme for life and health insurance to ens- ensure it's consistent and supports the policy goal of the proposed changes.

It's no net market benefit from these entities preparing climate statements given RBNZ's prudential, prudential supervision address already addresses climate risk, and the materiality assumptions for an audience that does not exist in the sense there's no primary users.
